name: Golden Horizon
description: A canvas flooded with amber and burnt gold, where a low sun dissolves into layered bands of ochre, rose, and deep copper. The land beneath is barely suggested, a thin dark line that anchors the blaze of light above it. Painted in the final ten minutes of a dying afternoon at the edge of the Rift Valley, the artist stood there for seven evenings before finding this exact light, one that feels less like a view and more like being held by something vast and ancient.

name: Midnight Abstract
description: Deep fields of prussian blue and violet build across this canvas in overlapping veils, each layer revealing traces of what was painted and scraped away beneath it. Thin channels of near-black open between the colour fields like fissures in deep water. Thirty-two layers were built and destroyed over three weeks. What remains is not what was planned, it is what survived.

name: Bloom Series I
description: A single flower fills the frame, its petals moving from deep magenta at the edges to a pale, luminous centre. The background falls to soft darkness, placing the bloom in a kind of spotlight that makes it feel both precious and impermanent. The artist photographs flowers at the exact moment they begin to turn, not wilting, not fully open, but caught in the strange grace between those two states.

name: Urban Silence
description: A wide city street stretches into predawn darkness, its wet surface holding pale reflections of distant streetlamps. No figures, no movement, only the geometry of buildings and the long quiet of a city not yet awake. The photographer walked this street every morning for a month before the light fell exactly right, a brief, cool stillness before the heat and noise reclaimed everything.

name: Fractured Light
description: At first glance this appears to be an abstract painting in warm gold and amber. Look closer and you begin to see real depth, glass and pigment suspended inside layers of clear resin that catch and bend whatever light falls on them. The surface shifts as you move around it, revealing colours and shapes that were invisible a moment before. A second work lives hidden inside the first.

name: Stoneware Vessel
description: A tall vessel with walls that taper from a wide shoulder to a narrow base, its surface matte and uneven, the ash glaze pooling darker in the recesses and lifting to a dry, sandy finish at the rims. The glaze was gathered from the artist's own kiln firings over two years. No two firings ever produce the same surface. The flame decides what the glaze becomes, and this one became something particularly still.

name: Forest Echo
description: Tall trunks recede into a dense interior of layered greens, each plane of foliage slightly darker than the one in front of it. The light does not come from above, it seems to emanate from within the forest itself, a diffuse luminosity that makes the space feel breathable. Painted from memory twelve years after the artist last stood in these trees, the colours were arrived at through failure and accident, not from life.

name: Neon Grid
description: Hard-edged rectangles and lines in electric violet, magenta, and cyan sit against pure black, each shape precisely placed so the colours vibrate against each other rather than simply coexist. The composition has the logic of a circuit board and the energy of a city seen from altitude at night. Drawn from satellite images of Nairobi at 2 a.m., from high enough every city becomes the same fever dream of light and geometry.

name: Still Life No. 7
description: Grapes heavy on the vine, split pomegranates, peaches with their skin still blushing, and a peeled orange unravelling at the base, all piled in the manner of a Dutch feast, on a stone ledge against a near-black ground. The light falls from one side and catches every surface differently: the wet flesh of fruit, the dull skin of leaves, the soft powder of a grape. Nothing here is accidental. Every object was chosen, placed, and studied before a single brushstroke was made.

name: Woven Study
description: Horizontal bands of undyed cream and warm wheat-brown wool alternate across this large textile, broken at intervals by vertical threads in rust and natural grey that create a quiet, irregular rhythm. The surface has a texture you want to touch, threads catching light differently depending on angle. The pattern was not designed in advance, it was discovered through six weeks of listening to the loom.

name: Coastal Morning
description: Pale blue and grey wash together at the top of the composition where sky meets sea, the horizon barely distinguishable from the water below it. The shallows resolve to green and white foam at the edge of wet sand, rendered in loose strokes that dissolve at their edges. The paper was damp from sea air when it was painted, the pigments spreading in ways that could not be planned or repeated.

name: Shadow Play
description: A sharp diagonal of shadow cuts across a sun-bleached concrete wall, its edge so precise it reads almost as a drawn line. The wall is featureless except for the shadow, which gives the image all its tension and all its beauty. The photographer followed this shadow across a building for an entire afternoon, waiting for the moment it reached its longest, most dramatic point.

name: Amber Resin Panel
description: Warm amber resin encases drifts of copper and gold pigment alongside fragments of 24k gold leaf, all suspended mid-fall as if caught by the material itself. The surface is glassy and smooth but the interior has depth, layers that shift in colour as light changes across the day. The amber tone was not chosen, it emerged from the resin reacting with the specific gold and the temperature of the studio that evening.

name: Verdant Study
description: A single leafy stem occupies the left of the canvas, its broad leaves rendered in close, observed detail, the veins visible, the slight curl at the edges, the underside paler than the top. The palette is deliberately restrained, soft sage, moss, and a touch of yellow-green. The artist returned to this same plant every few days over three months, painting it fifty times as it grew and changed in the light.

name: Geometric I
description: Two colours, deep purple and warm cream, divide this square print into interlocking triangles and rectangles fitted together with the precision of a puzzle. The forms are flat and without depth, existing as pure shape and colour on the page, yet the composition holds a strange spatial tension that pulls the eye in several directions at once. The edition is limited to 40, because the recurring dream that inspired it has only appeared 40 times.

name: Storm Study
description: The entire canvas is sky, dark churning blue-grey from edge to edge with no horizon, no ground, no escape from the weather. Thick impasto strokes move in opposing directions as if the paint itself is caught in wind, and pale streaks of near-white cut through the darker mass like lightning frozen mid-strike. The canvas was anchored with rope during the actual storm that produced it.

name: Porcelain Bowl
description: A low, wide bowl in pale celadon, its walls curving outward from a narrow foot to a generous open rim. The glaze is the colour of shallow seawater held in jade, pooling slightly deeper at the base where it settles into soft teal. The interior is smooth enough to eat from and beautiful enough not to. Fired three times before achieving this particular quiet.

name: Rose Noise
description: A large field of rose and coral bleeds across uncoated paper, its edges soft and irregular where the risograph ink spread beyond its intended boundaries. Within the field, darker passages accumulate in loose clusters that suggest petals without depicting them. The misregistration and bleeding are not defects, they are the work. The rose colour was not in the original design, the machine added it.

name: Monochrome No. 4
description: A woman stands inside a long corridor of white light, her figure a dark silhouette against the brightness behind her. The light falls from a source outside the frame, casting her shadow forward and filling the space with a silver luminosity that baryta paper renders with extraordinary tonal range. The scene lasted three seconds before she moved on. The photograph has been described as melancholy, as triumphant, and as a portrait of longing.

name: Cave Ochre
description: Raw canvas carries marks in deep ochre and iron red that feel older than painting, a handprint pressed into one corner, sweeping arcs that suggest animals in motion, clusters of dots whose meaning the viewer is left to decide. The pigment was ground by hand from raw mineral in near-darkness, asking what it would mean to make an image before language, before meaning, before art had a name.

name: Depth Field
description: An almost entirely dark canvas, the ground so deep it absorbs the eye completely. Within the darkness, a handful of marks in navy and charcoal describe something that might be a form, might be a space, might be nothing at all. The painting rewards slow looking, shapes that were invisible at first begin to emerge, then retreat again. Some people find it frightening. Others find it the most restful thing they have ever looked at.

name: Coral Study
description: A branching coral specimen rendered in warm watercolour washes of terracotta, salmon, and pale peach, its intricate geometry filling the paper with botanical precision softened by the fluid medium. The white of the paper glows through the thinner washes, giving the coral a translucence that makes it appear lit from inside. It was found bleached and dying after a storm, painted for four days, then returned to the sea.

name: Indigo Sky
description: Pure, uninterrupted blue fills the upper four-fifths of this wide photograph, so saturated and even it reads at first as colour field painting rather than sky. A thin strip of flat dark land bisects the lower frame, its simplicity making the expanse above feel infinite. The colour is not enhanced, that is the actual blue of the sky at that latitude, at that altitude, four hours from the nearest power line.

name: Deep Indigo
description: A large canvas saturated in natural indigo, its surface alive with subtle variation: darker passages where the dye pooled, lighter areas where the linen resisted, faint horizontal marks left by the application process that give the field a quiet rhythm. The blue changes across the day, warmer in morning light, shifting toward violet by evening. The artist was trying to paint the feeling of being homesick for a place she has never been.

name: Morning Haze
description: Soft grey dissolves into rose at the centre of this small watercolour, which in turn gives way to a pale gold at the edges, as if the paper itself has caught the light of the first hour of the day. There are no defined shapes, only colour in motion, the pigments allowed to find their own boundaries on damp paper. Made in forty minutes on a morning the artist could not sleep.

name: Emerald Landscape
description: Rolling hills in deep, saturated green fill the canvas from edge to edge, their curves soft under a pale overcast sky that takes up the upper third of the composition. The paint is applied with visible confidence, broad strokes that describe the lay of the land without overexplaining it. No figures, no roads, no human mark beyond the painting itself, made standing in an Aberdare field with six colours and one brush.

name: Pale Light
description: White linen curtains diffuse the morning light into something almost formless, a warm cream-grey softness that fills the frame with only the suggestion of a window and fabric behind it. The image is nearly abstract. The photographer was searching for a way to photograph the feeling of safety, of being somewhere quiet and known, and found it in light she had walked past every morning for years without seeing.

name: Ocean Series II
description: Gestural marks in cobalt, teal, and white move across the canvas in directions that mirror the push and pull of water, some strokes long and horizontal, others short and turbulent. The impasto surface catches light and shadow so the painting seems to shift when you move around it. The artist swims in the Indian Ocean every morning before painting, and this canvas was made immediately after, the rhythm of the water still in the body.

name: Rose Garden
description: Dozens of roses in full bloom crowd the frame, petals ranging from deep carmine at the centre to blush and cream at the edges, stems and leaves barely visible beneath the abundance of flower. The image has the overwhelmed feeling of a garden at its absolute peak, everything blooming at once, nothing held back. The garden no longer exists. The land was sold the following spring. The roses are here.

name: Golden Hour
description: A lone figure stands at the edge of a rooftop, backlit by a sun so low it turns the sky behind them into sheets of amber and deep orange. The figure is entirely in silhouette, gender and age unknowable, defined only by their stillness and the quality of light surrounding them. They did not know they were being photographed. The photographer has wondered ever since what they were thinking, what they were letting go of, or holding on to.
